description: Stage 3.5: branch on training state, run a late finisher tournament, and pick the best export-state portfolio from the winning branch on the executable strong 8192-tokenizer local base.

screen.description: Short event-branch tournament screen on deployed score.

decision.description: Long single-GPU runs for promoted event-branch strategies.

champion_8x.description: Full-box confirmation run for the best event-branch strategy.

slots.name: static_control_a
slots.validates: The strong default remains the reference point for event-branch deltas.
slots.falsifies: If control is unstable, branch-stage comparisons are suspect.

slots.name: static_control_b
slots.why: Noise-floor control with a different seed.
slots.validates: Short-horizon variance is small enough to judge event-triggered branching.
slots.falsifies: If controls spread too much, branch wins are not separable.

slots.name: prequant_ttt_tri_portfolio
slots.broken_invariant: Branch time should be chosen in advance and the export state should be fixed.
slots.mechanism: Trigger branching on late-state conditions, then run a tri-finisher tournament over narrow TTT, broad TTT, and discriminative-tail TTT programs and choose the best export mode within each branch.
slots.why: The real uncertainty is now mostly in the late deployment program, not just branch timing. This stage attacks both trigger timing and TTT-law selection.
slots.early_signal: Branching triggers before the failsafe max fraction and a nontrivial TTT branch/mode pair wins.
slots.failure_mode: Trigger is too noisy, branch budget gets diluted, or the TTT variants are too similar to separate.
slots.kill_rule: No screen gain vs control and best branch collapses to the same TTT recipe every time without a score win.
slots.validates: Event-triggered branching plus export-state selection beats a fixed single-path ending.
slots.falsifies: Late uncertainty is too small for branching and portfolio selection to matter.

slots.name: ttt_breadth_vs_depth
slots.broken_invariant: More branches are always better than deeper branches.
slots.mechanism: Use a tighter scale-gated trigger and only two deeper TTT finishers: freeze-2 TTT and discriminative-tail TTT.
slots.why: If the main value is branch depth, two stronger TTT programs should beat a broader but shallower tournament.
slots.early_signal: Winner separates clearly by screen and is not under-trained late.
slots.failure_mode: Dropping the narrow last-2 recipe loses the real late winner.
slots.kill_rule: No gain vs H501 or control by screen.
slots.validates: Depth per branch matters more than branch breadth.
slots.falsifies: Late branch breadth is still the dominant lever.

slots.name: plateau_gated_ttt_swing
slots.broken_invariant: Late aggressive finishers are too risky even if we can discard them.
slots.mechanism: Trigger on plateau or failsafe, then test aggressive broader-tail TTT and recurrent-deploy finishers against a conservative narrow TTT fallback.
slots.why: Branching should buy permission to swing harder in the late stage because bad TTT/deploy programs can be discarded.
slots.early_signal: A nontrivial aggressive TTT or recurrent-deploy branch wins at least some screens.
slots.failure_mode: Aggressive finishers destabilize and the stage falls back to the conservative narrow TTT branch.
slots.kill_rule: Best branch is always the conservative narrow TTT branch and no gain vs control.
slots.validates: Branching enables materially harder late swings.
slots.falsifies: Even with discardable branches, conservative endings dominate.

slots.name: export_state_style_ttt
slots.broken_invariant: Late policy matters more than export-state style.
slots.mechanism: Use event-triggered branching but keep the TTT program fixed while competing on export-state style: raw-state TTT, EMA-state TTT, and checkpoint-selected TTT.
slots.why: This isolates whether the dominant uncertainty is the starting/export object rather than the TTT law itself.
slots.early_signal: One state-style wins consistently across branches.
slots.failure_mode: All state styles are too similar and selection adds only overhead.
slots.validates: Export-state style is a first-order late decision.

slots.name: ttt_vs_recurrent_deploy_duel
slots.broken_invariant: The trivial EMA branch is necessary as a late fallback.
slots.mechanism: Event-trigger into a two-way duel between broader dTTT-tail adaptation and recurrent deploy shaping.
slots.why: If the branch stage is real, it should be able to discriminate between nontrivial TTT and deploy stories directly.
slots.early_signal: One mechanism-heavy branch wins cleanly without needing EMA fallback.
slots.failure_mode: Removing the conservative narrow branch makes the branch set brittle.
slots.kill_rule: Both branches lose to control or to H501.
slots.validates: Differentiated late mechanisms are the true source of value.
slots.falsifies: EMA remains the only robust fallback and should stay in the set.

slots.name: failsafe_ttt_event_tri
slots.broken_invariant: Branch timing should be either fully adaptive or fully fixed.
slots.mechanism: Use an event trigger with a strong max-frac failsafe to guarantee a late TTT/deploy tournament even if the event never fires.
slots.why: This tests whether event sensitivity plus a hard safety branch point is more robust than a purely adaptive or purely fixed trigger for modern late programs.
slots.early_signal: Some runs trigger early while others fall back to the failsafe, and the stage still improves deployed score.
slots.failure_mode: The trigger remains effectively fixed-time and gains little from adaptivity.
slots.kill_rule: No gain vs H501 or control, or trigger behavior collapses to one pattern.
slots.validates: Hybrid event-plus-failsafe branching is more robust than one trigger style alone.
slots.falsifies: Adaptive trigger logic does not buy useful robustness.
